content.text: Every robot policy trained purely in simulation faces the same fundamental problem: no simulator captures reality with perfect fidelity. Friction coefficients differ, actuator dynamics are idealized, contact forces are computed with simplified models. The moment the policy meets real hardware, it enters conditions it has never seen — and often fails. Domain randomization is the technique of making a policy deliberately robust to this mismatch by training it across a broad distribution of simulation conditions, so that the real world becomes just one more sample from a distribution the policy has already learned to handle.
What is it?

Domain randomization (DR) is a training strategy in which simulation parameters are varied randomly across episodes. Rather than training with fixed physics parameters, each episode draws new values for quantities like friction, object mass, joint damping, gravity scale, actuator delay, and — in visual DR — texture, lighting, and camera geometry. The policy must learn behavior that works across all these variations. By making the simulation space large enough to contain the real world as a member, the policy generalizes from simulation to reality.
DR was popularized by OpenAI's
Dactyl

project (2019), which trained a five-fingered robot hand to manipulate a Rubik's Cube in MuJoCo with aggressive randomization of friction, joint stiffness, timestep delays, and visual appearance. The resulting policy transferred to the physical Shadow Hand without any real-world data collection. Prior to DR, sim-to-real for dexterous manipulation had required extensive real-world fine-tuning or was simply not feasible.
Why it matters

The alternative to DR is to build an extremely accurate simulator — one that matches real hardware so precisely that the policy transfers without modification. This approach (system identification) is expensive, time-consuming, and brittle: the model must be re-identified whenever the hardware changes, a component wears, or environmental conditions shift. For high-DOF systems like dexterous hands or legged robots, the parameter space is large enough that perfect system identification is practically impossible.

DR takes the opposite stance: instead of reducing simulation error to zero, it trains the policy to be robust to simulation error. This converts the sim-to-real problem from a precision engineering problem into a generalization problem — and generalization is something deep RL is already well-equipped to handle.
Benefits
No real-world data required.
Policies can be trained entirely in simulation and deployed directly on hardware, which is crucial when real-world data is expensive or dangerous to collect.
Robustness to hardware variation.
A policy trained with DR degrades gracefully when hardware parameters change due to wear, temperature, or manufacturing tolerance — not abruptly as with policies trained on fixed parameters.
Enables perception-based policies.
Visual DR with randomized textures and lighting can train vision-based policies that transfer to real camera images without any real-world supervision.
Scales well.
DR is nearly free computationally — sampling new parameters at episode reset costs microseconds — and is trivially parallelizable across simulation instances.
Orthogonal to other techniques.
DR can be combined with system identification, domain adaptation, and residual learning. It is not an either/or choice.
Trade-offs & limitations
The central tension in DR is between
coverage and learnability

. Wider randomization ranges make the distribution more likely to contain the real world, but also make the task harder to learn. If friction is randomized from 0.01 to 5.0, the agent must learn to grasp objects that feel anywhere from ice-slick to sandpaper in the same episode — a far harder task than learning to grasp at a fixed friction of 1.0.
Overly broad distributions degrade performance.
If parameter ranges are set too wide, the policy may learn conservative or sub-optimal strategies that work across all conditions rather than performing well in any specific one.
Setting ranges requires expert knowledge.
Without characterization of real hardware variance, it is unclear how wide each parameter range should be. Ranges too narrow fail to cover reality; too wide hurt policy quality.
Uniform distributions are naive.
Real parameters are not uniformly distributed around their nominal values. Gaussian or log-normal distributions are often more accurate, but harder to set without real data.
Visual DR can overfit simulation artifacts.

Randomizing textures with flat colors or procedural noise does not reproduce the structured appearance of real scenes. Vision policies may still fail on real-world clutter not covered by the distribution.
Increases training time.
A policy trained with DR typically needs more environment interactions to converge than one trained on fixed parameters, because the effective task difficulty is higher.
How it works
DR is implemented at the environment level. At each call to
env.reset()

, the environment samples new values for each randomized parameter from its configured distribution, updates the simulator model, and starts the new episode with these values. The policy never sees the parameter values directly (unless they are part of the observation) — it must infer appropriate actions from the observation alone.
There are three standard distribution types.
Uniform distributions
(most common) sample from [low, high] with equal probability — easy to set and interpret.
Gaussian distributions
center around the nominal value with a tunable standard deviation, better matching physical manufacturing tolerances.
Log-uniform distributions
are appropriate for parameters that span orders of magnitude (e.g., gear ratios, damping coefficients), ensuring that small and large values are sampled with equal probability in log space.
A more sophisticated variant is
structured DR

(also called automatic DR or curriculum DR), where the ranges are expanded gradually as training progresses. The agent starts with easy, near-nominal conditions and the distribution widens over millions of steps. This avoids the cold-start problem where an untrained policy cannot learn from very diverse conditions.
Architecture overview

attrs.alt: Domain Randomization architecture: three blocks (Physics Randomization, Visual Randomization, DR Distributions) each feed a central Robust Policy block. The policy is trained across the union of these distributions and therefore transfers to the real robot.

content.text: The diagram illustrates the three pillars of domain randomization. Physics DR randomizes dynamics parameters per episode. Visual DR randomizes appearance. The distribution type (uniform, Gaussian, log-uniform, curriculum) determines the shape of each parameter's variation. These three sources of variation together produce a robust policy that handles the real world as one point in the combined parameter space.
When to use it
Use DR
when training entirely in simulation with no real-world data, especially for manipulation, locomotion, or any high-DOF task where system identification is impractical.
Use visual DR
when training perception-based policies (camera input) and collecting labeled real-world images is not feasible. It is the standard approach for sim-to-real in object detection and grasping.
Combine with system identification
when you have some real hardware access: use sysid to center the DR ranges around measured values, then randomize around those centers.
Avoid DR
when the task requires maximum performance at a single operating point. DR imposes a cost on peak performance in exchange for robustness.
How to implement
Implementing DR in MuJoCo requires modifying the model's
mjModel
fields at each reset. The model stores physics parameters as flat NumPy arrays (e.g.,
model.geom_friction
model.body_mass
model.dof_damping
). Overwriting these at reset applies the new parameters to all subsequent simulation steps.
Examples

# Example 1: Physics domain randomization with MuJoCo
# pip install mujoco gymnasium numpy

import mujoco
import numpy as np
import gymnasium as gym
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Tuple


@dataclass
class DRConfig:
    """Ranges for physics domain randomization."""
    # Friction: [sliding, torsional, rolling]
    friction_range: Tuple[float, float] = (0.5, 2.0)
    # Mass multiplier applied to all non-world bodies
    mass_scale_range: Tuple[float, float] = (0.8, 1.2)
    # Joint damping multiplier
    damping_scale_range: Tuple[float, float] = (0.5, 2.0)
    # Gravity scale (nominal = 1.0 -> 9.81 m/s^2)
    gravity_scale_range: Tuple[float, float] = (0.9, 1.1)
    # Actuator delay in steps (0 = no delay)
    actuator_delay_steps: Tuple[int, int] = (0, 3)


class DomainRandomizedEnv(gym.Env):
    """Wraps a MuJoCo env with episode-level domain randomization."""

    def __init__(self, model_path: str, dr_config: DRConfig = None):
        self.model = mujoco.MjModel.from_xml_path(model_path)
        self.data = mujoco.MjData(self.model)
        self.dr = dr_config or DRConfig()

        # Save nominal values for reference
        self._nominal_friction = self.model.geom_friction.copy()
        self._nominal_mass = self.model.body_mass.copy()
        self._nominal_damping = self.model.dof_damping.copy()

        # Action / observation spaces (placeholder; override in subclass)
        nq = self.model.nq
        nv = self.model.nv
        nu = self.model.nu
        self.observation_space = gym.spaces.Box(-np.inf, np.inf, shape=(nq + nv,))
        self.action_space = gym.spaces.Box(-1.0, 1.0, shape=(nu,))

    def _apply_domain_randomization(self, rng: np.random.Generator):
        """Sample and apply randomized physics parameters."""
        # Friction
        friction_scale = rng.uniform(*self.dr.friction_range)
        self.model.geom_friction[:] = self._nominal_friction * friction_scale

        # Body mass (skip world body at index 0)
        mass_scale = rng.uniform(*self.dr.mass_scale_range)
        self.model.body_mass[1:] = self._nominal_mass[1:] * mass_scale

        # Joint damping
        damping_scale = rng.uniform(*self.dr.damping_scale_range)
        self.model.dof_damping[:] = self._nominal_damping * damping_scale

        # Gravity (Z component only; X and Y stay zero)
        gravity_scale = rng.uniform(*self.dr.gravity_scale_range)
        self.model.opt.gravity[2] = -9.81 * gravity_scale

    def reset(self, seed=None, options=None):
        super().reset(seed=seed)
        rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)

        # Apply domain randomization before resetting simulation state
        self._apply_domain_randomization(rng)
        mujoco.mj_resetData(self.model, self.data)

        obs = np.concatenate([self.data.qpos.copy(), self.data.qvel.copy()])
        return obs.astype(np.float32), {}

    def step(self, action):
        self.data.ctrl[:] = np.clip(action, -1.0, 1.0)
        mujoco.mj_step(self.model, self.data)
        obs = np.concatenate([self.data.qpos.copy(), self.data.qvel.copy()])
        reward = 0.0   # Override in subclass
        return obs.astype(np.float32), reward, False, False, {}

# Example 2: Visual domain randomization for perception-based grasping
# Randomizes texture, lighting color, and camera position using a custom renderer

import numpy as np
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Tuple


@dataclass
class VisualDRConfig:
    """Configuration for visual domain randomization."""
    # RGB texture: each channel sampled uniformly from [lo, hi]
    texture_rgb_range: Tuple[float, float] = (0.2, 0.9)
    # Light intensity [0, 1]
    light_intensity_range: Tuple[float, float] = (0.4, 1.0)
    # Light hue offset in degrees [-30, 30]
    light_hue_range: Tuple[float, float] = (-30.0, 30.0)
    # Camera position jitter in meters
    camera_pos_jitter: float = 0.05   # std dev for Gaussian jitter
    # Camera FoV in degrees [40, 80]
    camera_fov_range: Tuple[float, float] = (50.0, 70.0)
    # Background texture index (int) drawn from library
    num_background_textures: int = 100


class VisualDomainRandomizer:
    """Applies visual DR to a MuJoCo model at reset time."""

    def __init__(self, model, config: VisualDRConfig = None):
        self.model = model
        self.cfg = config or VisualDRConfig()

    def randomize(self, rng: np.random.Generator):
        """Apply all visual randomizations. Call at each env.reset()."""
        self._randomize_textures(rng)
        self._randomize_lighting(rng)
        self._randomize_camera(rng)

    def _randomize_textures(self, rng: np.random.Generator):
        """Set all non-skybox material colors to random RGB."""
        # model.mat_rgba has shape (nmat, 4): R G B A
        n_mats = self.model.nmat
        for i in range(n_mats):
            r, g, b = rng.uniform(*self.cfg.texture_rgb_range, size=3)
            self.model.mat_rgba[i] = [r, g, b, 1.0]  # keep alpha=1

    def _randomize_lighting(self, rng: np.random.Generator):
        """Vary light intensity and hue offset."""
        intensity = rng.uniform(*self.cfg.light_intensity_range)
        # model.light_diffuse has shape (nlight, 3)
        n_lights = self.model.nlight
        for i in range(n_lights):
            base_rgb = np.array([1.0, 1.0, 1.0])
            hue_shift = rng.uniform(*self.cfg.light_hue_range) / 360.0
            # Apply a simple hue rotation approximation
            hue_rgb = np.array([
                0.5 + 0.5 * np.cos(2 * np.pi * hue_shift),
                0.5 + 0.5 * np.cos(2 * np.pi * (hue_shift - 1/3)),
                0.5 + 0.5 * np.cos(2 * np.pi * (hue_shift + 1/3)),
            ])
            self.model.light_diffuse[i] = np.clip(base_rgb * hue_rgb * intensity, 0, 1)

    def _randomize_camera(self, rng: np.random.Generator):
        """Jitter camera position and field of view."""
        n_cams = self.model.ncam
        for i in range(n_cams):
            jitter = rng.normal(0, self.cfg.camera_pos_jitter, size=3)
            self.model.cam_pos[i] += jitter
            self.model.cam_fovy[i] = rng.uniform(*self.cfg.camera_fov_range)

# Example 3: Structured (curriculum) DR - progressively widen ranges
# Inspired by OpenAI Dactyl's automatic domain randomization approach

import numpy as np
from typing import Dict, Tuple


class CurriculumDR:
    """
    Automatic domain randomization: start narrow, widen ranges based on
    policy success rate. When success rate > threshold, expand the range.
    When success rate < lower_threshold, contract it.
    """

    def __init__(self, params: Dict[str, Tuple[float, float, float, float]]):
        """
        params: {name: (nominal, min_range, max_range, step_size)}
        Starts at range = 0 (nominal only), expands toward max_range.
        """
        self.params = params
        # Current half-width of each parameter's range
        self.widths: Dict[str, float] = {k: 0.0 for k in params}
        self.success_history: list = []
        self.expand_threshold = 0.7   # expand if success > 70%
        self.contract_threshold = 0.3 # contract if success < 30%
        self.window = 100             # rolling window size

    def sample(self, rng: np.random.Generator) -> Dict[str, float]:
        """Sample one set of parameters from the current ranges."""
        result = {}
        for name, (nominal, lo_limit, hi_limit, step) in self.params.items():
            width = self.widths[name]
            lo = max(lo_limit, nominal - width)
            hi = min(hi_limit, nominal + width)
            result[name] = float(rng.uniform(lo, hi))
        return result

    def update(self, success: bool):
        """Record episode outcome and possibly adjust ranges."""
        self.success_history.append(float(success))
        if len(self.success_history) < self.window:
            return  # not enough data yet

        recent = self.success_history[-self.window:]
        rate = sum(recent) / len(recent)

        for name, (nominal, lo_limit, hi_limit, step) in self.params.items():
            if rate > self.expand_threshold:
                self.widths[name] = min(
                    self.widths[name] + step,
                    max(abs(hi_limit - nominal), abs(nominal - lo_limit))
                )
            elif rate < self.contract_threshold:
                self.widths[name] = max(0.0, self.widths[name] - step)


# Usage
cdr = CurriculumDR({
    "friction":   (1.0, 0.3, 3.0, 0.05),
    "mass_scale": (1.0, 0.5, 2.0, 0.02),
    "damping":    (1.0, 0.2, 5.0, 0.1),
})
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
params = cdr.sample(rng)
print(params)  # Initially all at nominal: {'friction': 1.0, 'mass_scale': 1.0, ...}
cdr.update(success=True)  # After 100+ episodes, ranges start widening
Real-world example
OpenAI's
Dactyl

(2019) applied DR to over 100 physics parameters including joint stiffness, joint damping, object size, object mass, friction, surface normal direction, and tendon properties. Critically, it also randomized observation noise (adding Gaussian noise to finger tip positions) and action delay (introducing random lag between commanded and applied torques). The automatic domain randomization variant progressively expanded these ranges during training, starting from near-nominal parameters and expanding as the policy's success rate improved. The final policy transferred to a real Shadow Hand and solved the Rubik's Cube using only fingertip position sensors — no wrist cameras.

A separate study by Tobin et al. (OpenAI 2017) demonstrated visual DR for robot grasping. By training a CNN object detector purely on simulated images with randomized textures, lighting, and distractor objects, the resulting detector transferred to real-camera images of a physical robot bin without any real-world training data. The key finding was that random, unrealistic textures worked just as well as photorealistic textures for transfer — the model learned features robust to visual variation rather than features that matched any specific appearance.
Common mistakes
Setting ranges too wide from the start.
If friction is randomized from 0.1 to 10 on the first episode, the policy often fails to learn at all because the task is impossible at the extremes. Start narrow and widen.
Forgetting to randomize observation noise.
Real sensors are noisy. A policy that has only seen clean simulation observations will be over-confident on real noisy sensor data. Add Gaussian noise to joint angle and force readings.
Ignoring actuator delay.
Every real motor controller introduces lag between commanded and achieved positions. Not randomizing this delay produces policies that assume instantaneous response and fail on hardware.
Treating all parameters independently.
In reality, parameters are correlated (a heavier object has more inertia and more friction). Sampling independently can produce physically implausible configurations that the real world never visits.
Using flat random textures for structured scenes.
Random solid-color or noise textures do not cover structured real-world scenes with text, logos, or objects in the background. Supplement with real image patches as backgrounds.
Best practices
Measure real hardware variance first.
Use system identification or datasheets to set the center and initial width of each DR range. Guessing ranges without measurement often results in ranges that miss reality entirely.
Use curriculum DR for high-DOF tasks.
Start at nominal parameters and widen automatically as the policy succeeds. This is more sample-efficient than training from the full distribution.
Randomize observation noise and action delay.
These are among the most impactful parameters for real-hardware transfer and are often overlooked in physics DR implementations.
Log the randomized parameters alongside training metrics.
This enables ablation studies: which parameters matter most for transfer? Which ranges can be reduced without hurting robustness?
Test transfer before full deployment.
Before running the policy on the full robot task, test it on a controlled hardware bench with known parameters. If it fails there, the DR ranges do not cover real hardware.
Performance & scalability

DR adds negligible computational cost to the simulation step itself — parameter sampling and model field updates take microseconds. The main cost is in sample efficiency: a policy trained with DR requires more episodes to converge than one trained on fixed parameters, because the effective task difficulty is higher. Empirically, 2x–5x more samples are typical when adding aggressive physics DR.
DR scales linearly with parameter count.
Adding more randomized parameters generally increases the sample cost proportionally. Prioritize the parameters with the most impact on real-world variance.
GPU parallelism amplifies DR.
With 1000 parallel Isaac Sim environments each using a different DR sample, you effectively see the entire distribution in every training batch — optimal for coverage.
Visual DR is more expensive.
Rendering randomized textures and lighting per episode is significantly more costly than physics DR. Isaac Sim's photorealistic DR is GPU-only and requires more memory per environment instance.
Tooling & ecosystem
Tool
Purpose
Notes
MuJoCo mjModel fields
Direct physics DR via model array modification
Fastest approach; modify geom_friction, body_mass, dof_damping
IsaacGym / Isaac Lab
GPU-parallel DR with 1000+ envs, built-in randomization API
Best for large-scale physics DR; Python API with PhysX backend
NDDS / BlenderProc
Photorealistic synthetic image generation for visual DR
NVIDIA Dataset Synthesizer and BlenderProc produce labeled sim images
robosuite
Manipulation benchmark with built-in DR wrappers
Good reference implementation; wraps MuJoCo with randomization API
PyBullet envs (pybullet_envs)
Physics DR via resetJointState and changeDynamics
p.changeDynamics() updates friction, mass, lateral friction at runtime

Security & reliability
Policies trained with DR may appear robust in simulation evaluations while still failing on the real robot if the DR ranges do not cover the true hardware distribution. This is a
silent reliability failure

: simulation metrics look good, real-world deployment fails. The danger is more acute for safety-critical applications (surgical robots, autonomous vehicles) where simulation-only validation is insufficient.
Always test on real hardware before safety-critical deployment.
Simulation evaluation with DR does not guarantee real-world performance. Run hardware tests with the actual robot in the actual task environment.
Validate DR ranges against hardware measurements.
Periodically re-characterize hardware parameters (especially after maintenance) and verify they fall within the DR distribution.
Add safety constraints to the policy output.
Even a robust DR-trained policy should be wrapped with joint velocity and torque limits that are enforced at deployment, preventing catastrophic actions from rare out-of-distribution states.
Key takeaways
Domain randomization trains a policy across a distribution of simulation variants so that the real world becomes just one more sample the policy can handle.
Physics DR (friction, mass, damping, gravity) and visual DR (textures, lighting, camera) address different aspects of the sim-to-real gap; both are typically needed for perception-based manipulation.
Ranges too wide degrade policy performance; ranges too narrow fail to cover reality. Curriculum DR (progressively widening ranges) is the safest approach for new tasks.
Randomize observation noise and actuator delay alongside physics parameters — these are critical for real-hardware transfer and often neglected.
OpenAI Dactyl (2019) demonstrated zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for dexterous manipulation by combining aggressive physics DR with automatic range widening across 100+ randomized parameters.
DR is not a substitute for hardware testing in safety-critical applications; it reduces the gap but never eliminates the need for real-world validation.
